speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Ignac Feldman. So when exactly did the war end for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, wait, wait, wait. Still I remember I have a group. And when I see to save life only how much from the civil people and I see you have a good friend like [? Hibenuke. ?] He was shot at Ercsi. It is 36 kilometers south of Budapest. And his stomach was, like, open. But he was alive. And we cannot help him because the fire was so hot, so thick, we cannot come.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the night, I know where he fell. I come with, with Liashchenko, my friend. And I put his coat. We closed his coat to keep it inside him. And we put here a fire and schlepped him out from danger till it comes the [INAUDIBLE]. Where are the Liashchenkos, the Grebeniuks, and the others? Liashchenko survived. I survived. And the other nice people was falling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we have people fall. We have new people in that time to teach them how to go on with the dynamite. But the one man I see, he couldn't. And I tell them, take him away. Because he would kill himself. And the other people were taking out the mines. But [INAUDIBLE]. Then I tell my people listen, stay away from him 25 yards. And once, I didn't know his name, I give a look. He was flying in the air. He blew up for carelessness. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we come close to the end of the war-- the 8th of May-- yes, like I take-- I told you. I go to this [NON-ENGLISH  SPEECH]. And I saw that there was Jewish homes-- Jewish population in the city. This was a Jewish settlement-- Jewish city.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you living as a goy, you know this is [INAUDIBLE]. They are full with Jewish life. You'll not see a Jewish lawyer [INAUDIBLE]. You'll a not see a Jewish home. You see on the home the slogan, we cleaned, clean up Slovakia from, from the, from the Jewish people. How was this said?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then comes to the 8th of May. We're still fighting. Because on our-- the other side front was keeping the [? Vlasov, ?] these, these [? men, ?] the Russian deserters who go again-- went over to the Germans. They was afraid for the Russians, they will be punished. And the SS, they all know the regular may give over. But they was fighting. Because they knew what's they are responsible.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the 9th of May-- 10 of-- the 8th of May-- everything was quiet down. And then from [INAUDIBLE], I wrote to my wife a letter. What is to do? I think I will be free. And I can go all over the world. But she told me, if you are free and can go over the world, come over where I am with the child. And we'll go together 7,000 kilometers.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was so good to travel with hardship. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I traveled to Moscow. They give hotels because as, as a front man, as a fighter. [? Then ?] I didn't know to cry or to laugh till I come very close to the city, to Bukhara. It was-- I passed Tashkent and [INAUDIBLE] and Kogon. Then after 12, 15 kilometers-- and the train was-- you know, during the war, everything was not comfortable and nice. We have nowhere to sit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am with my luggage. But I see this time another [INAUDIBLE]. This was like a [INAUDIBLE], like a committee. I see sitting a high-ranking officer. And there is place free. Because everybody is afraid to sit near him. Then I go in, walk to him. And I ask him, is the place free, citizen? He look on me. He said, yes. I sit down. He asked me, what do you have in your luggage? Because they like [INAUDIBLE] to catch. I told different things. He asked me what? I have-- how's that-- for me-- private things for me, for my wife, for my child, different things. He asked me, you are from, from far away? Yes. And what he asked me, I answered him. Because I was not afraid. And he asked me, in [INAUDIBLE] you was? Yes. In [INAUDIBLE] you were? Yes. [INAUDIBLE] From where you have so many things? Where did you brought that? I told him some places I bought. Some places I took, like, like a soldier on the front. Then he opened his gun, you see? Then I asked him a simple question. Tell me, please, where is [INAUDIBLE], the street, 3A? Then he asked me-- and he has a address ready. He make a note. Why you ask? I told him because my wife with a child is there. Then you don't know where is it? No. Then from where you came? I told him, please, read-- take out the address that [INAUDIBLE] in the foreign address that they gave. Then he salute me [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't know. I have 3,500 Polish Jewish people. Nobody was on the front. And you was on the front. I am sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they take me. And they see I couldn't walk straight. He took the luggage. He bring-- he asked me, maybe you will go in our hotel-- I will bring you in our hotel. And you will sleep over until morning, because it's very dangerous to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The city is in Asia. It's not central Russia. I told, listen, if will be not seeing your, your family, your wife, your child, three and a half years, you come back on front. You will go to hotel, or you will go to the wife? He told, yes, you are right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But wait, he bring me to the, to his building. He grabbed two agents. He tell one will keep the luggage. The other will keep me. And I have to [? sign. ?] And everything is OK. And I came to my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: I come in. [? The soldiers ?] did the same thing. I tell them be careful. I tell them a friend from her husband. And she [? noted in Russia, ?] who? Then she asked me, where is-- [INAUDIBLE] my husband. Who give the regard? And tell him I am here. And she looks. And then I come in. And this was my son, was five and a half years old, leaving with 23 months. He tell, is it really my father? But I tell him, listen, I cannot take you till I go to wash myself. Because I was traveling seven weeks or eight weeks on the road. Now I must take a good, good shower, you see? They said they a bath and changed everything [INAUDIBLE]. Because I'm afraid for sickness. And so it was. And then we was sin Poland. Yes, then I can go straight back. Let me just interrupt you for a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said some time ago that your wife was pregnant with a second child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have a second child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. About eight months, she had the second. I know when-- but she had it on the floor without help, without anything. And take her up into the hospital. And she was very sick. She had blood poisoning. There was no medication. It was a miracle. And I noticed, I know at the time. And I came off [? Ukraine ?] at this time as a soldier in a home. I only had a couple of hours to stay. And there was a child named Anatol, like my son. What I had, I give everything to the child. It was so hard. Anyways--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to the child?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died. And she, she was in the hospital. And Anatol was with her also in the hospital. Because they were sitting-- because there was nowhere to go. And then, then I come back to Bakhara. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the friend what was mobilized, he was, he was in the workings battalion, not on the front. But his wife knows where, where she-- he is. Meantime, he was with the other woman. The woman had a child from him. And she comes to me and to write to Iosif.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even I told to my wife, I cannot write. Why? She has no a child. Now, he is with another woman. She has a child. How can I take him away and tell her like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: But still, she was so kind. Then I wrote him a letter. Listen, Iosif, I am with my Lonia, with my Anatole. Your wife is so crying. You are not here. You know, he comes back once. It take him a month or two-- two months.

speaker: interviewee
text: He comes [INAUDIBLE] with my letter and asks him with my wife, where's Ignac to see me? Because I helped him a lot when he was-- when I was working [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] And they liked me, because I was a good specialist. And here, a young man comes in and asks for work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of the directors asked me what specialty. I told, he has no specialty. He is young. He tells me, whatever, he can-- no work, no bread there in Russian. When I hear that, I tell him to the, to the leaders from the, the, the manager from the [INAUDIBLE], from the factory. I told him, listen, stop [INAUDIBLE]. I told him, listen, he's a young guy. He can take. He can be at the sit to [INAUDIBLE] something to do. Give him work. He's a young guy. A few things like that, he [? decide ?] to help him with. OK, a few things like that. He call him back to give him [INAUDIBLE]. And when I go there to [? evacuate ?] in the collective farm, he is coming with me too, with his wife. And when I wrote a letter, then he comes over. My conscious wasn't clear. Because I was-- I know he has a woman with a child. And the other woman sent to his wife a letter. I'm his wife. He is with me. We have a child. And you look for another husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tarlowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Iosif Tarlowski. [INAUDIBLE] And he is now in, in Holon, a big building in Israel. He was a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you and your wife do at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, when I came to, to Bakhara, they called me in to the voenkomat. It's a military institution. They tell, listen, you like-- what do you like to do? I tell them like to stay a few months because to go back, they put-- the [INAUDIBLE] see I cannot go. Because they put the army against Japan. Then I must go to the Trans-Siberian Road around [INAUDIBLE]. I told them I'm afraid my child is now three, fours years in middle Asia-- a warm climate. I'm afraid to go now back. I'd like to be over the winter in the spring to go home. Tell, OK, we'll give you a job. They gave me a as a from a restaurant so to make a living, because you are a man who was fighting [INAUDIBLE]. And you helped us a lot. OK. And I was working there till the time comes in, in June, I think. At the end of May, 1946, I go back to Poland. As soon as we come back to Poland, I see we met the Polish people already what-- yes. Show us, we will kill you. After this, everything-- and I told my wife, I don't like to be in Poland [INAUDIBLE]. Like to go out. It was two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of your family survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Really, I make-- when I talk with you, I make a mistake. Because we were more than seven children. My father has a stepson. And, and he adopted him. He was the first [? Gemir ?] Feldman. Then he has two children his own-- Nadja and Berek. And then with my mother because my mother was the first marriage, but my father the second marriage, he has Yehuda, Fela, I, [INAUDIBLE] and Cela, five. And it was together eight. And we've [INAUDIBLE]. [? Gemir ?] was a soldier [INAUDIBLE] a very religious man. He has four sons. He-- nobody survived from the family-- not he, not his wife, not the four sons. Nobody survive. My sister, Nadja, she married Abe Laib Weizman. Then she have a beautiful daughter, Anja. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know which year they took her out from the Lodz ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nobody survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then there is my brother, [? Dov ?] Feldman. He was in the Lodz ghetto with his wife with two children. And thanks God, he survive. He bring the children from one place to the other. He hides them. And he survived. Then Yehuda, he is-- he was married to [? Lola Siegelthorpe-- ?] a very beautiful girl with red hair. And they-- all the time, they had no children. And then they got there was born a child. I hear-- I don't know exactly-- when they take them out to Auschwitz, she tore out the child, the girl, the baby, and they hand to a Polish woman. But I don't know which station. And I don't know the name of the woman. So it's like you take-- you drop something in [? a river of water, ?] you cannot find. From Fela with me and Yehuda was receive a letter 1946 from my cousins from Russia. He comes back. And he tells me, listen, this is a letter from your sister, [? Anja ?] and Fela. She wrote to us. There were no-- no surviving. The youngest sister, Cela, she was writing, writing also to the same cousin. [INAUDIBLE] this letter. I lost. Maybe my wife put it away. Then I never see the letter, because every time when I saw the letter, it struck me on the heart. [CRYING] She wrote, I am 13 years old. I like so the life, but if to die, she [? not ?] and I am with, with the gun, at least on the road like my brother, not like that. Maybe she put it away. With the years, now she cannot find this letter. And this is the life. I am sitting here remembering, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sad. If people will came and will tell, never was a Holocaust, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe they will tell that Stalingrad was only a play. If they can tell that, they can tell this also was never in the history of mankind was a fight like that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know. But I hope man can learn. And when I hear that my father comes back 1922, after eight and a  half years, with the hope never war. Because he passed this. I come back 1945 with the same hope-- no more-- God forbid, special in this time. Especially I was afraid not for the illiterate people. I was afraid for the cultured, [? the revered ?] people, people who give out Wagner. People who gave us Schiller. The people who was wrote the Nibelungs. When they can fall so low and kill children, I don't know what to think. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Only sometimes, I will ask, yes, on my way to-- on my way from, from Amsterdam to Canada, I was on a boat, on the boat And I had a [INAUDIBLE], a philosophical book Spinoza's in my hand-- Baruch Spinoza. You know  the discussion and how he was excommunicated from Jewish life. I was even in the synagogue [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And across from me sitting by the table was sitting a man-- he, he observed me. It was a priest, a minister. He asked me, Baruch Spinoza? I told him, yes. Then he asked me, you are from the survivors? I told him, yes. He asked my opinion about the Germans . I told him, no, I have nothing against the Germans. I do not hate the Germans. Then who? I tell him, I will give you only a sample. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In the history from the Greeks, we have there a little history passed away two people, 5,000, 6,000 years ago, the mythology. One was a criminal, a murderer and the other a righteous man. But according to the law in the heaven, the criminal, he passed away 10 minutes before, 15 minutes before. So he was there when the called the first, and then the righteous man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he staying for the call, they asked him, who are you? What is your name? He started to go out from his name. They said, we know who you are. Even the hell is no good. You must go between the sky and the planet. And we will find a punishment. We will call you back, because up to date, we not have punishment for you. Then not the other guy, the righteous man, they asked him who you are. And [? tell him, ?] ah, you are the righteous man. Yes. What you did on the planet? I told them, I am-- was a righteous man. I was looking for righteousness for the law and everything. They asked him a simple question. You know who was before you? Oh, yes, this criminal, this [INAUDIBLE]. They look at him and tell him, you know, immoral sin, you are more responsible than he. Because he was born to be like he is. But you, you was a righteous man. You know he had a knife. Why didn't you take out the knife? Then I tell him, I was afraid. How you can become righteous and be afraid for that? When you see when killed people, innocent people, then you was afraid. You don't have the, the place in the law to be in heaven. He looked on me, what do you mean? I tell him I mean the Vatican. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hate it. If with the Vatican-- Pius XII will tell, don't kill innocent children, women, because Jesus was a Jew. Don't kill Jesus' brothers and sisters. But he was afraid for who? If he was afraid, then he has not a right to be. You know, he was crying. He tell, nobody, nobody give me this to understanding like you showed, like even from the history a little bit, you see? Then he understand that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, so is life. You see, now you hear in  the papers this wasn't true. And this wasn't true. And this wasn't true. I don't know-- it's been deep in my heart when I see the-- from hundreds families, nobody survived, even [INAUDIBLE] not survive-- like from my cousins, from my mother's side, from my father's side. It is, it is the story. I like something must be learned from that. I like children in the schools supposed to know that, not to hide how hard the truth is. But give it to them to children and [? keep ?] children. Let them know what was our past. OK, thank you for listening.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're not finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after you left Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: After we left Poland, we went to Germany, in Heidenheim. We came to Germany through Austria in Germany. And we went in-- to Heidenheim. In Heidenheim, I start working as employment officer in the UNRRA. But that was a short time, only 18 months. Because I cannot take it. This was a long time. And the first immigration that was open was Norway and Holland. So I chose Holland and come to Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have family there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We have-- when we arrived was around two o'clock the, in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- when was this? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in Amsterdam I think in 19-- [INAUDIBLE] 1951, no, 1947, [INAUDIBLE] 1947, and I think in December, was it in? And comes people on the station to us from this city for this city, survivor [? was. ?] And comes one gentlemen. And he asked a simple question. of we know a family from-- who is from the city Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he comes to our wagon. I and my wife are from Lodz. And he asked a simple question, oh, we know maybe in Lodz the family Tenenbaum? And I look on him. And  I tell him, if I know the family, then you are Mr. [? Zeide. ?] Then he asked me, how you know that? I tell him, I was with your sister-in-law, a good friend. She has a library, [INAUDIBLE] library.

speaker: interviewee
text: And your wife, she was in vacation this and this year-- 1937. And I was-- 1937, I was in the army. So right away, he comes home. He was very good. Here's the situation. And tomorrow, he took this wife. And they come. We were sitting together. He has-- they help us a lot out [? even. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I stop you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, please.
